vocabulary.hint: Character Breakdown: 你 -> 亻 (person) + ⺈ + 小 (small)
Character Breakdown: 好 -> 女 (woman) + 子 (child)
Character Breakdown: 您 -> 亻 (person) + ⺈ + 小 (small) + 心 (heart)
Character Breakdown: 你 -> 亻 (person) + ⺈ + 小 (small) | 们 -> 亻 (person) + 门 (door)
Character Breakdown: 对 -> 又 ((once) again) + 寸 (a unit of length) | 不 -> 一 (one) + 丿 (radical in Chinese characters (Kangxi radical 4)) + 卜 (surname Bu) | 起 -> 走 (walk) + 己 (self)
Character Breakdown: 没 -> 氵 (water) + 殳 (weapon) | 关 -> 丷 ("eight" component in Chinese characters) + 一 (one) + 大 (big) | 系 -> ㇒ + 糸 (silk)

grammar.explanation: 您 is the polite form of 你 (nǐ), used to show respect when addressing elders, teachers, or superiors.

grammar.point: Personal Pronouns (你, 我, 他, 她, 它)
grammar.explanation: Chinese personal pronouns are straightforward. Plurals are formed by adding 们 (men) to the singular form.

examples.english: They are not going to school.

vocabulary.hint: Character Breakdown: 谢 -> 讠 (speech) + 身 (body) + 寸 (a unit of length) | 谢 -> 讠 (speech) + 身 (body) + 寸 (a unit of length)
Character Breakdown: 不 -> 一 (one) + 丿 (radical in Chinese characters (Kangxi radical 4)) + 卜 (surname Bu)
Character Breakdown: 不 -> 一 (one) + 丿 (radical in Chinese characters (Kangxi radical 4)) + 卜 (surname Bu) | 客 -> 宀 (roof) + 夂 ("walk slowly" component in Chinese characters) + 口 (mouth) | 气 (gas)
Character Breakdown: 再 -> 一 (one) + 冂 (radical in Chinese characters (Kangxi radical 13), occurring in 用, 同, 网 etc) + 土 (earth) | 见 (to see)

grammar.explanation: 不 is the most common way to negate verbs and adjectives. Note: When followed by a 4th tone, 不 changes to 2nd tone (bú).

examples.english: He is not a teacher. (Tone change to bú)

vocabulary.hint: Character Breakdown: 叫 -> 口 (mouth) + 丨 (radical in Chinese characters (Kangxi radical 2)) + ㇙
Character Breakdown: 什 -> 亻 (person) + 十 (ten) | 么 -> 丿 (radical in Chinese characters (Kangxi radical 4)) + 厶
Character Breakdown: 名 -> 夕 (dusk) + 口 (mouth) | 字 -> 宀 (roof) + 子 (child)
Character Breakdown: 我 -> 手 (hand) + 戈 (surname Ge)
Character Breakdown: 李 -> 木 (wood) + 子 (child) | 月 (meat)

Character Breakdown: 老 (prefix used before the surname of a person or a numeral indicating the order of birth of the children in a family or to indicate affection or familiarity) | 师 -> No glyph available + 一 (one) + 巾 (cloth)
Character Breakdown: 学 -> ⺍ + 冖 ("cover" radical in Chinese characters (Kangxi radical 14), occurring in 军, 写, 冠 etc) + 子 (child) | 生 (life)
Character Breakdown: 人 (person)

grammar.explanation: 什么 is used to ask 'what'. In Chinese, the word order of a question is the same as a statement—you simply replace the noun with 什么.

examples.english: What do you want to eat?

grammar.explanation: The verb 是 is used to link a subject with a noun or noun phrase (A is B). It cannot be used to link a subject with an adjective.

vocabulary.hint: Character Breakdown: 她 -> 女 (woman) + 乚 (component in Chinese characters) + ㇆ + 丨 (radical in Chinese characters (Kangxi radical 2))
Character Breakdown: 谁 -> 讠 (speech) + 隹 (bird)
Character Breakdown: 的 -> 白 (white) + 勹 (archaic variant of 包[bao1]) + 丶 (see 點|点[dian3])
Character Breakdown: 汉 -> 氵 (water) + 又 ((once) again) | 语 -> 讠 (speech) + 一 (one) + 力 (surname Li) + 一 (one) + 口 (mouth)
Character Breakdown: 哪 -> 口 (mouth) + ㇆ + 二 (two) + 丨 (radical in Chinese characters (Kangxi radical 2)) + 阝 (city/mound)
Character Breakdown: 国 -> 囗 (enclosure) + 玉 (jade)
Character Breakdown: 他 -> 亻 (person) + 乚 (component in Chinese characters) + ㇆ + 丨 (radical in Chinese characters (Kangxi radical 2))

Character Breakdown: 同 -> 冂 (radical in Chinese characters (Kangxi radical 13), occurring in 用, 同, 网 etc) + 一 (one) + 口 (mouth) | 学 -> ⺍ + 冖 ("cover" radical in Chinese characters (Kangxi radical 14), occurring in 军, 写, 冠 etc) + 子 (child)
Character Breakdown: 朋 (friend) | 友 -> 𠂇 + 又 ((once) again)

grammar.point: The Interrogative Pronouns 谁 (shéi) and 哪 (nǎ)
grammar.explanation: 谁 means 'who' and 哪 means 'which'. Like 'what', they occupy the same position in the sentence as the word they replace.
的 is used to indicate possession or a descriptive relationship. It follows the possessor/modifier.

vocabulary.hint: Character Breakdown: 家 -> 宀 (roof) + 豕 (pig)
Character Breakdown: 有 -> 𠂇 + 月 (meat)
Character Breakdown: 口 (mouth)
Character Breakdown: 女 (woman) | 儿 (son)
Character Breakdown: 几 (small table)
Character Breakdown: 岁 -> 山 (mountain) + 夕 (dusk)
Character Breakdown: 了 -> ㇇ + 亅 ("vertical stroke with hook" radical in Chinese characters (Kangxi radical 6))

Character Breakdown: 今 -> 人 (person) + 丶 (see 點|点[dian3]) + ㇇ | 年 -> 丿 (radical in Chinese characters (Kangxi radical 4)) + 一 (one) + 十 (ten) + ㇗ + 丨 (radical in Chinese characters (Kangxi radical 2))
Character Breakdown: 多 (many)
Character Breakdown: 大 (big)

grammar.explanation: 几 is used to ask 'how many' for small numbers (usually expected to be less than 10). It is almost always followed by a measure word.

examples.pinyin: Nǐ jiā yǒu jǐ kǒu rén?
examples.english: How many people are in your family?
How many cups do you have?

grammar.explanation: When used at the end of a sentence, 了 indicates a change in situation or the occurrence of a new state.

examples.english: I am not going to school anymore.

vocabulary.hint: Character Breakdown: 会 -> 人 (person) + 二 (two) + 厶
Character Breakdown: 说 -> 讠 (speech) + 丷 ("eight" component in Chinese characters) + 口 (mouth) + 儿 (son)
Character Breakdown: 妈 -> 女 (woman) + 马 (horse) | 妈 -> 女 (woman) + 马 (horse)
Character Breakdown: 菜 -> 艹 (grass) + 爫 (claw) + 木 (wood)
Character Breakdown: 很 -> 彳 (step) + 艮 (mountain)
Character Breakdown: 好 -> 女 (woman) + 子 (child) | 吃 -> 口 (mouth) + 丿 (radical in Chinese characters (Kangxi radical 4)) + 一 (one) + ㇠
Character Breakdown: 做 -> 亻 (person) + 十 (ten) + 口 (mouth) + ⺙
Character Breakdown: 写 -> 冖 ("cover" radical in Chinese characters (Kangxi radical 14), occurring in 军, 写, 冠 etc) + No glyph available + 一 (one)

Character Breakdown: 读 -> 讠 (speech) + 十 (ten) + ㇖ + 大 (big) + ⺀

grammar.explanation: 会 indicates an ability acquired through learning (know how to).

examples.pinyin: Wǒ māma huì zuò Zhōngguó cài.
examples.english: My mother can cook Chinese food.

grammar.explanation: 怎么 is used before a verb to ask about the 'how' or 'manner' of an action.

examples.english: How do you write this character?
How do you say your name?

vocabulary.hint: Character Breakdown: 请 -> 讠 (speech) + 青 (blue)
Character Breakdown: 问 -> 门 (door) + 口 (mouth)
Character Breakdown: 今 -> 人 (person) + 丶 (see 點|点[dian3]) + ㇇ | 天 -> 一 (one) + 大 (big)

Character Breakdown: 月 (meat)
Character Breakdown: 星 -> 日 (sun) + 生 (life) | 期 -> 甘 (sweet) + 一 (one) + 八 (eight) + 月 (meat)

Character Breakdown: 书 -> No glyph available + 丨 (radical in Chinese characters (Kangxi radical 2)) + 丶 (see 點|点[dian3])

grammar.explanation: A sentence where the predicate is a noun, number, or noun phrase, often used for dates, time, weather, or age. No verb (like 是) is strictly required.

grammar.point: Sentence with a Serial Verb Construction: 去 + Place + Action
grammar.explanation: Used to express going to a place to do something. Order: Subject + 去 + Location + Verb.

examples.english: I go to school to read.
He goes to China to study Chinese.
We go to the store to buy things.

Character Breakdown: 那 -> ㇆ + 二 (two) + 丨 (radical in Chinese characters (Kangxi radical 2)) + 阝 (city/mound)

grammar.explanation: 想 is used before a verb to express a desire or a plan ('would like to', 'want to').

examples.english: I would like to drink tea.
What would you like to buy?
I want to go to school tomorrow.

grammar.explanation: 多少 is used to ask for a number or quantity, usually when the expected answer is greater than 10. Unlike 几, a measure word is optional.

examples.english: How many students are in your school?
How many books do you have?

grammar.explanation: 在 functions as a verb meaning 'to be at/in/on'. Sentence structure: Subject + 在 + Place.

examples.english: My friend works at the hospital.
The kitty is under the chair.

grammar.explanation: Used at the end of a sentence to ask about the location of someone or something (where is...?).

grammar.explanation: Used to state that something exists in a certain place. Structure: Place + 有 + Object.

examples.english: There is a computer on the table.
There is a store in the school.
There is a dog under the chair.

grammar.explanation: 能 indicates possibility or permission ('can', 'may').

examples.pinyin: Wǒ néng kàn nǐ de shū ma?
examples.english: May I look at your book?

grammar.explanation: In Chinese, time is expressed starting from the largest unit to the smallest. 点 (diǎn) = hour, 分 (fēn) = minute.

grammar.explanation: Used to ask about the condition of something or a situation ('How is...?', 'What about...?').

examples.english: How will the weather be tomorrow?

grammar.explanation: Used to express an excessive degree (too...). It often carries a subjective tone.

grammar.point: The Particle 呢 (ne) for Action in Progress
grammar.explanation: Used at the end of a sentence to indicate that an action is currently happening (ongoing state). Often used with 正在 (zhèngzài) or 在 (zài).

examples.english: He is making a phone call.

grammar.explanation: When placed after a verb, 了 indicates the completion of an action. It is often used for past events but describes 'completion', not 'tense'.

examples.english: She bought quite a few clothes.

grammar.explanation: Used to emphasize the time, place, or manner of an action that is known to have already occurred.

examples.pinyin: Wǒ shì zuò fēijī lái de.
examples.english: I came by plane. (Emphasis on manner)
We met in the morning. (Emphasis on time)

examples.chinese: 他是在北京大学学习的。
examples.pinyin: Tā shì zài Běijīng Dàxué xuéxí de.
examples.english: He studied at Peking University. (Emphasis on place)
